name: Cotton T-Shirt
description: A conventional cotton tee — from field to landfill (and the loops that can interrupt that path).

stages.details: Cotton is grown on large monocultures, often irrigated heavily. Fibre is harvested mechanically, ginned to separate seed from lint, then baled for spinning mills.

stages.summary: Spin, weave, dye, and sew the garment
stages.details: Lint is spun into yarn, knitted or woven into fabric, dyed (often with wet chemistry), cut, and sewn. Finishing may include softening, printing, or packaging.
Garments move through ports, distribution centres, and retail (or e-commerce last-mile). Packaging and returns amplify the footprint of a low-cost item.
Fast-fashion tees are often worn few times then discarded. Landfill and incineration dominate when collection systems are weak or fibre blends make recovery hard.

stages.summary: Extend life via second-hand and repair
stages.details: Donation, thrift, clothing swaps, and simple repairs (seams, holes) keep the shirt in use. Reuse usually beats recycling on energy and water.
Mechanical recycling shreds cotton into lower-grade fibre (often blended). Chemical recycling can dissolve cellulose into new pulp but remains limited at scale for post-consumer cotton.
